questions.question_text: In an Iowa contract for deed, who holds legal title during the payment period?
distractors: The buyer holds legal title
Title is held in escrow
The county recorder holds title
questions.explanation: Under an Iowa contract for deed, the seller retains legal title to the property until the buyer completes all payments. The buyer receives equitable title and possession, but legal title does not transfer until the contract is fulfilled.

questions.question_text: A buyer purchases a farm under a contract for deed but does not record it. What risk does the buyer face?
questions.correct_answer: A later buyer could claim superior title
distractors: The contract becomes automatically void
The seller cannot enforce the contract terms
The buyer loses possession of the property
questions.explanation: Recording a contract for deed protects the buyer's equitable interest by providing constructive notice. Without recording, a subsequent good-faith purchaser could potentially claim superior title under Iowa's recording act (Iowa Code 558.41), which protects subsequent good-faith purchasers for value without notice.

questions.question_text: A contract-for-deed buyer makes all payments and fulfills all terms. What must the seller provide?
distractors: A release of mortgage lien
A certificate of occupancy
An abstract of title only
questions.explanation: Upon full payment under a contract for deed, the seller is obligated to deliver a deed transferring legal title to the buyer. This is the fundamental promise in a contract for deed: the buyer pays the full price, and the seller conveys legal ownership.

questions.question_text: A seller agrees to finance a property sale using a contract for deed. What does the buyer receive at execution?
distractors: Legal title and possession
A deed and mortgage
Only a lease with option to buy
questions.explanation: In a contract for deed, the buyer receives equitable title and the right to possess the property at the time of execution. Legal title remains with the seller as security until the purchase price is paid in full, at which point the seller delivers a deed.

questions.question_text: Why should a buyer record a contract for deed with the county recorder in Iowa?
questions.correct_answer: To protect the buyer's interest with public notice
distractors: Recording is required for the contract to be valid
To transfer legal title to the buyer
The seller cannot sell without it recorded
questions.explanation: Recording a contract for deed provides constructive notice of the buyer's equitable interest to the public. While the contract is valid between parties without recording, recording protects the buyer against subsequent claims by third parties.

questions.question_text: Under Iowa law, what is the minimum notice period a seller must give before forfeiture of a contract for deed?
distractors: 10 days
60 days
90 days
questions.explanation: Iowa Code chapter 656 requires the seller to serve a written notice giving the buyer 30 days after completed service to perform the terms in default and pay the reasonable costs of serving the notice. This applies to all Iowa real estate contracts; for agricultural land with $20,000 or more outstanding, the seller must first obtain a farm mediation release.

questions.question_text: A buyer under an Iowa contract for deed misses two monthly payments. The seller serves a forfeiture notice. What right does the buyer have?
questions.correct_answer: Right to cure default during notice period
distractors: No rights once the notice is properly served
Right to extend payments by an additional year
Right to demand a completely new contract
questions.explanation: After receiving a forfeiture notice, the Iowa buyer has the right to cure the default by making all past-due payments plus costs during the notice period. If the buyer cures within the statutory timeframe, the contract remains in effect.

questions.question_text: How does forfeiture of a contract for deed differ from foreclosure in Iowa?
questions.correct_answer: Forfeiture is faster with no court action needed
distractors: Forfeiture requires a full court proceeding first
Foreclosure is always faster than forfeiture process
There is no practical difference between the two
questions.explanation: Forfeiture of a contract for deed is generally faster than foreclosure because it does not require filing a lawsuit or obtaining a court judgment in many cases. Foreclosure involves a judicial process with potentially longer timelines and greater costs.

questions.question_text: A contract-for-deed buyer in Iowa receives a 30-day forfeiture notice and pays all amounts owed on day 25. What is the result?
questions.correct_answer: The contract is reinstated in full
distractors: The forfeiture still proceeds as scheduled
The buyer must renegotiate all contract terms
The seller may reject the payment and proceed
questions.explanation: When the buyer cures the default within the statutory notice period by paying all amounts owed plus applicable costs, the contract for deed is reinstated. The seller cannot proceed with forfeiture once the buyer has properly cured.

questions.question_text: An Iowa seller wants to forfeit a contract for deed on agricultural land with an outstanding balance of $25,000. What must the seller generally obtain before initiating forfeiture?
questions.correct_answer: A mediation release under the farm mediation law
distractors: A court judgment of foreclosure first
The buyer's written consent to the forfeiture
An appraisal establishing the buyer's equity
questions.explanation: Iowa Code section 656.8 bars forfeiture of a contract for agricultural property with an outstanding obligation of $20,000 or more unless the seller first obtains a mediation release under section 654A.11, or a court finds the delay would cause irreparable harm. Otherwise, Iowa forfeiture requires only the 30-day notice procedure — there is no equity threshold that forces a seller to foreclose instead.
